entry.title: Cheat Sheet for Unix CLI
entry.content:
Learnt Unix in NUS CS2030S. Here's a cheatsheet.

Original Text Here

Background

  • Ctrl + D : end of input/ exit shell

  • Ctrl + Z : suspend the current running program. This pauses the execution of the program (but not terminates it). Resume executing in the background with the command bg Back to execution in the foreground again with the command fg.

  • Ctrl + C : terminate the current running program.

  • Ctrl + S : freeze the terminal. Ctrl + Q : resume the terminal.

  • Ctrl + P & Ctrl + N: See the previously executed commands.

  • Tab : complete the name of a command/file

  • man : look at the manuals. e.g. : man ls

Essentials

Directory

Symbols

  • ~ : The home directory. ~bob means the home directory of a user named bob.

  • . : Current working directory.

  • /: Root directory.

  • .. : Parent directory.

Path

  • Absolute Path: /home/o/ooiwt

  • Relative Path: ../o/ooiwt

  • pwd : Print current working directory.

  • ls : Check the list of contents. Contents that starts with . are hidden, use ls -a to check all contents. ls -F to see more information. ls -l to see information including the size and date.

  • mkdir : make a directory

  • cd : brings you to the home directory

Files

  • rmdir : remove a subdirectory, it must be empty.

  • cp :

    cp -r copys the whole directory: $ cp -r ../workshop .

* If there is an existing file with the same name, cp will overwrite the existing file without warning.

  • mv : Rename a content.

    When the destination of mv is a directory, then it moves the source to the destisnation

    It's better to use mv -i as it asks if you want to overwrite a file

  • rm : removes the file.

    *It's better to use rm -i.

  • cat : Take a look at the content of a file.

    less : look at less content of a file.

      In less, use `space` to move forward,  `b` to move back, `q` to quit.

File Permission

What:

  • r : read - 4

  • w : write - 2

  • x : executing a file / access contents and info of the directory - 1

Permission are used in two ways:

  1. rwx or r-x etc.

  2. Numerical notation like : 5 = r-x

Who

u is the user who owns the file; g refers to the users in the same group as the user; and o are all the other users.

For instance, the permission of 644, or rw-r--r--, on a file means that:

- the owner can read and write

- the group users can only read

- all the other users can only read

Check and Change of Permission

Check:

$ ls -l test.txt
-rw-r--r--@ 1 ooiwt  staff  64 Jul 28 09:52 test.txt

Change:

$ chmod 666 test.txt
$ ls -l test.txt
-rw-rw-rw-@ 1 ooiwt  staff  64 Jul 28 09:52 test.txt

Or:

$ chmod o-w test.txt
$ ls -l test.txt
-rw-rw-r--@ 1 ooiwt  staff  64 Jul 28 09:52 test.txt

Eliminates the writing permission of a user.(use o+w to give the permission)

Standard Input & Output

  • wc : it will count the number of lines, words, and characters in that given file. e.g.:
  • > & >> : The operators > and >> are used to redirect the standard output to a file. The difference is that > will overwrite the given file, while >> will concatenate into the given file. e.g.:

This programme reads the output of wc text.txt into a file test.count

  • < & << : Imports the input.

Advanced

To be continued.

entry.title: Notes for CS2040S
entry.content:
Mainly questions that I encountered during my study for CS2040S

Lecture 1 & 2 : Java

  1. [U] How to implement a File System?

1. Objects represent state (“nouns”), not actions (”verbs”)

2. What after folders?

3. Objects should be unitary, without dividing functionality.(What does this mean and why?)

  1. First Principal of JAVA: Everything is an object

    A class is a template for producing an object.

    An object is an instance of the class.

  2. Second Principal of JAVA: Everything has a type

    Type String:

    • charAt(int index)

    • substring(int begin, int end)

    • toCharArray()

    • length()

    • compareTo(String otherString)

    • compareToIgnoreCase(String otherString)

    • equals(Object anObject)

  1. Behaviour is public, Data is private.

    regular variables/functions are PER OBJECT

    static variables/functions are PER CLASS // cannot access member variable

Lecture 3 & 4 : Peak Finding & Searching

  1. [U] 2D Peak Finding: How to PROVE that it works?

Lecture 5 & 6 : Sorting

BogoSort

  1. BogoSort Running Time

What is the expected running time of BogoSort?

Ans: O(n * n!) // The n is important!

  1. Quantumn BogoSort

Remember QuantumBogoSort when you learn about non-deterministic Turing Machines. What?

  1. Maybe BogoSort

MaybeBogoSort(A[1..n])

[U]What is the expected running time of MaybeBogoSort?

I guess it's something like 
    $\sum_{k = 1}^{n} (k-1) * (k-1)!$

[U]But then, What is the bigO notation of this thing?

[U]Additionally, what is the difference between n! and \(n^{n}\)? What is the difference between n * n! and (n+1)! ?

Stirling Apporximation:

by this approximation, we can say that `O(log(n!)) == O(nlog(n))`
  • [U]
  1. [U] List of the stability, runtime and performance difference on nearly sorted and random arrays of different algorithms

Classic Sorting Algorithms

BogoSort

Selection Sort

Insertion Sort

In this case, since the insertion work is done from nearest element to key(backwards), it behaves differently in a sorted array

[U] Average-case analysis:

On average, a key in position j needs to move j/2 slots backward (in expectation).(WHY?)

MergeSort

[U] Master Theorem? Akra–Bazzi Method ? ## Summary

Features Bubble Sort Selection Sort Inerstion Sort Merge Sort
Time Complexity O(n^2) O(n^2) O(n^2) O(n * log(n))
NearlySorted ? Random O(n) < = O(n) < =
Space Complexity O(n) O(n) O(n) O(n) (No need to allocate new arrays, can you previous ones instead) / O(n * log(n)) when it's MergeSort iteration
Stability 1(swap only when elements are different) 0(e.g. [2, 2, 1]) 1(A[i] > key not >=) 1(if implementation is designed carefully)

[U] Find a permutation of [1..n] where:

• BubbleSort is slow.

• InsertionSort is fast.

Or explain why such array does not exist

How do you count space?

• Maximum space every allocated at one time?

• Total space ever allocated.

[U] How “close to sorted” should a list be for InsertionSort to be faster?

Slowest Sorting Algorithm

Quicksort

Introduction

  1. What if there are duplicates?

[U] Two way partitioning (what happens for numbers on the right side? Lec 6 slides)

One way partitioning - maintaining 4 parts of an array. * Quicksort is not stable

[U] Is it possible to make quicksort stable by using an extra array to partition rather than do swapping?

  1. [U] Why is quicksort faster than merge sort?

  2. [U] What if we use triple pivots

Time complexity for random chosen pivot.

Analysis on a good pivot

T(n) = T(n-k) + T(k) + n

Suppose \[\frac{1}{10} n < k < \frac{9}{10} n\]

Time complexity: O(n * h), where h is the time of partition

\[(\frac{9}{10}) ^ {h} * n >= 1\tag{1}\]

  • Paranoid Quicksort(partition until a good partition)

[U] Non-paranoid quicksort works too?

[U] Why is it more important to fool the adversary then for the algorithm to behave well on average cases(we would need time to do randomization and find the A[k], but we don't need time to get A[1]) Or, since the possibility of choosing a bad pivot is small, is the efficiency of doing paranoid quicksort better then randomly choosing?

Probability Theory on choosing a good pivot

We only execute the repeat loop \(\frac{n}{k}\) times (in expectation) 1

Quicksort Optimization

  1. Switch to InsertionSort for small arrays.

  2. Halt recursion early, leaving small arrays unsorted. Then perform InsertionSort on entire array. (InsertionSort is fast on almost sorted arrays, about O(n))

Order Statistics

Find \(k^{th}\) smallest element in an unsorted array:

  1. sort and return A[k]
  2. Minimize sorting

You can even do paranoid sorting

Time Complexity: O(n)

Others

  1. log2n = O( log10n ) Correct! We can easily switch between bases by multiplying by a constant.

Puzzles:

  1. [U]Sicherman Dice

  2. [U]Puzzle of the Week: Bubbly (Courtesy: MIT Puzzle Hunt, 2019)

  3. [U]Treasure Island:

You choose a set of keys.

You can test whether all the good keys are in the set (and possibly some extra keys). If even one bad key is in the set, then it fails.

Goal: identify exactly the good keys.

  1. PotW: Gambling for Profit? (Lecture 07 p121)

** Updated: Lecture 08 p23

  1. \(\color{green}{SOMETHING RELATED}\): How many flips to get at least one head? : \[E[X] = \frac{1}{2}(1) + \frac{1}{2} (1 + E[X]) \tag{1}\] (this is useful for infinity E[x] problems)↩︎

entry.title: Machine Learning Introduction on Coursera
entry.content:
Took the Machine Learning Specialization on Coursera, here's the notes.

Supervised Machine Learning: Regression and Classification

Week 1:Introduction to ML

Supervised VS Unsupervised Learning

  1. Supervised Learning

  • Input - Output prediction

    1. Train your models with x and correct answers Y

    2. Models take brand new X and predict Y

  • Regresion : predict X-Y mappings, where Y has unlimited inifinte possible values

  • CLassification: predict only a small number of outputs Y (categories/classes)

  1. Unsupervised Learning
  • Find something interesting (structure) in unlabeled data. Do not have an aim(e.g. clustering: (google news cluster the related news togethe / grouping different customers); Dimemsionality reduction ; Anomally depression)

Regression model

1. Terminology

  • Training Set: Data set used to train the model
  • x : Feature / input variable
  • y : "target" variable(the correct answer)
  • (x,y) : single training variable
  • (\(x^{(i)}\), \(y^{(i)}\)) : the ith row of training example
  • m : number of training examples
  • f : hypothesis / model
  • \(\hat{y}\) : prediction
  • \(f_{w,b}\)(x) = wx + b w, b: coefficients / weight
  • Univariate Linear Regression: one variable

2. Hands on pactice:

3. Cost Function

  • error: \(\hat{y^(i)}\) - \(y^(i)\)

\[J(w,b) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{w,b}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 \tag{1}\]

[U] Why is some places in the graph dense while others are loose? Does that imply anything?

Gradient Descent

Implemetation

\[\begin{align*} \text{repeat}&\text{ until convergence:} \; \lbrace \newline \; w &= w - \alpha \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial w} \tag{1} \; \newline b &= b - \alpha \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial b} \newline \rbrace \end{align*}\]

gradient can be defined as:

\[ \begin{align} \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial w} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{w,b}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x^{(i)} \tag{2}\\ \frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial b} &= \frac{1}{m} \sum\limits_{i = 0}^{m-1} (f_{w,b}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \tag{3}\\ \end{align} \]

\(\alpha\) : learning rate (determines how big a step would be) (always positive)

Change w by a little step to get closer to the min.

Repeat until convergence (w and b no longer changes much)

That is to say:

if a is too small, gradient descent is slow;

if a is too large, gradient descent may overshoot(never reach minimum), or fail to converge, or even diverge

If we use an appropriate fixed \(\alpha\) , then as we approach local minimum, the steps become smaller because the derivative gets closer to zero

Simultaneous update :

  • Why? Because we want the value of w and b to be the same when calculating the derivative.

Batch Gradient descnet: for each step of the gradient descent, we use all the training examples

Code

Functions to use:

  • compute_gradient implementing equation (2) and (3) above
  • compute_cost implementing \(J(w,b)\)
  • gradient_descent, utilizing compute_gradient and compute_cost

Code Convention: \(\frac{\partial J(w,b)}{\partial b}\) will be dj_db.

The 'quiver plot': The arrow sizes reflect the magnitude of the gradient at that point. The direction and slope of the arrow reflects the ratio of ∂𝐽(𝑤,𝑏)∂𝑤 and ∂𝐽(𝑤,𝑏)∂𝑏

Now, we can implement Gradient Descent:

  • The path makes steady (monotonic) progress toward its goal.
  • initial steps are much larger than the steps near the goal.

[ ] Todo: should learn python

[ ] Todo: clean the code above

TBC.

entry.title: General Studying Tips
entry.content:
General study tips.

  1. Use Red pen for knowledge that I don't remember when looking back; Blue pen for additional knowledge

entry.title: Thought - 2024.1
entry.content:
Thougts in Jan 2024

1.23 : Sleep is important.

1.24 : 有舍有得，但也需分轻重。

1.25 : 在图书馆而非宿舍学习。

1.26 : 允许自己犯错。

1.27 : Push yourself.

1.28 : Try everything!

1.29 : YOLO

1.30 : 严于律己，宽于待人。

1.31 :...So you have to trust that the dots will somehow connect in your future. You have to trust in something - your gut, destiny, life, karma, whatever. - Steve Jobs

entry.content:
This is a Blog to test this website's functionalities.

URL

markdown cheatsheet

latex Cheatsheet

code

useful syntax

Here's a sentence with a footnote. 1

This is a quote.

\(\color{blue}{blue}\)

Create a new post

More info: Writing

mathjax testing

\[\begin{equation} E = mc^2 \end{equation}\]

\[\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} a &= b + c \\ &= d + e + f + g \\ &= h + i \end{aligned} \end{equation}\]

\[x+1\over\sqrt{1-x^2} \]

  1. This is the footnote.↩︎

entry.title: Thoughts for Attending Guest Lecture on Pi
entry.content:
Attending a guest lecture is fun # Thoughts

I never thought about attending guest lectures before. Mainly because all the guest lectures that I attended in high school didn't talk much about "knowledge", but rather about "the history of knowledge" or the guests' experiences. Since we needed to compete for Olympiad and prepare for College Entrance Exams, we didn't have much time for doing "useless" stuff other than studying directly for the exams(at least, that's what I thought, I'll probably write about that as well in another post. If I have time). So I've always been kind of resistant to guest talks.

But then, I've started to change my mind since I came to university in a different country and tried to touch on things that I didn't want to take before(I'll also talk about that in the other post). Plus, I read Cal Newport's How To Win At College (I know this book sounds uitilitarian but it's actually good), in which he suggested listening to guest lectures twice a week. So I've decided to give it a try on a Maths Talks, since I want to know whether my interest actually lies in this realm.

The talk was given by prof Chan Heng Huat, from the Department of Mathematics in NUS. There were also several other profs who attended the meeting such as my prof from GEA1000N (What a surprise that I would be seeing him tmr) and profs from non-mathematics departments. Apart from this, several kids were also there in the talk, listening carefully and silently. That actually surprised me and I was even kind of jealous of those kids who get the chance to get exposed to these interesting talks when they were young(maybe one of them would be the next Ramanujan, who knows!).

Prof Chan mainly talked about the progress and various kinds of techniques or thoughts that were related to apporximating Pi. He used simple language, but dived deep into the thought progress of doing these approximations and gave useful details about the questions that were raised during the talk. The talk lasted for 40 minutes. It wasn't useful in a sense that I would probably forget about all these series and integrations next morning when I wake up. However, I would definetely say that it makes me remember why and how I loved Mathematics when I was young. I can't sum up now about what the talk brought to me or what I've learned in the talk, but I know in my heart that something was changed in my mind. Something was envoked. I cannot tell what it is right now, nor do I know how this would influence my future careers. I can't even say that it is "useful".

But as I am writing this, I remember David Brooks's talk about how everyone has this part of them that does not care about fame or money or tiktok likes, there is a part of us that pursues true happiness and higher realm. I guess I begin to remember how I like Maths as Maths itself, not because "Studying Maths Sounds So Hardcore" or "Maths Is Useful for CS" or anything like that. And I guess that's what guest lectures are for. To guide you to a higher perspective and envoke interest. To put down the utilitarian thoughts and discover something new. To truly enjoy the thing you love and remove the impurities imparted by reality to those things.

I look forward to attending more guest lectures.

This lecture is not a Special one. I wouldn't say that the lecture is the-thought-provoking-one. But as a first guest lecture that I take the initiative to participate, and as a first guest lecture that I attend in university, it does reminds me of something deep in my heart and inspires me to attend future guest lectures.

(Useless) Notes

Here are the notes:(taken real quick so basically not useful lah)

entry.title: Thought - 2024.2
entry.content:

2.18 : Making progress!

2.21 : guest lecture is fun.

entry.title: Welcome to my blog
entry.content:
\(\color{cyan}{Welcome To My Blog!}\)

Introduction

I've been trying to document things about myself, as a reminder that I have existed in this world.

In this blog, I might write about personal feelings, reading notes, thoughts, achievements, plans, challenges...etc

Welcome to my blog!

Have A Look Around!

  • To view \(\color{green}{daily thoughts}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/thoughts/}{<Here>}\)

  • To view \(\color{green}{notes}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Notes/}{<Here>}\)

  • To view some \(\color{green}{interesting challenges}\) that I've done, click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Challenges/}{<Here>}\)

  • To view some \(\color{green}{Experiences}\), click \(\href{https://wfyishere.github.io/categories/Experiences/}{<Here>}\)

Introduction to Functionalities of the Website

It's adviced to view this website on your computer(to fully make use of the functionalities)

## Sidebar

### Menu

On the left side of the website, the side bar, you can click on the different directories on the menu such as About, Categories, Tags, Archives, To Do, Search

  • About : Gives basic information about the website and me.

  • Categories : Page where you can check the different types of posts, such as Notes/Thoughts/Challenges etc. Subcategories are provided.

  • Tags : Labels different posts such as "MachineLearning" etc.

  • Archives : Overview of all the posts on the website in time order.

  • To do : Plans for what I want to write / functions that I want to implement in the future. Feel free to give advice!

  • Search : Search specific contents that you want to look for.

### Table of Contents

When you view an article, you can click on the sessions under Table of Contents to go to different chapters of the post.

### Globe

If you're using a laptop, you might notice the 'Globe' at the bottom of the sidebar. This shows the places where all the visitors of the website came from! Try put your cursor on the globe and zoom in/out using your mouse! Each red dot symbolizes one user!

## Posts

  1. Click on read more to read full content of each post!

  2. Word count and estimated Reading time are displayed under the title of each post.

  3. If you find my post interesting, feel free to click on the social media icons at the end of each post to share this post!

  4. You can login your github account to comment each post.

## Bottome Bar

The bottom bar provides information about the total wordcount / estimated reading time / the days this website has survived.

Thank Next & Hexo for powering this website.

entry.title: 吹灭读书灯，一身都是月
entry.content:
吹灭读书灯，一身都是月，多美的句子啊。

小的时候最喜欢的房子就是：

小小的房间，与世人的喧嚣隔绝。屋子里的东西不多，一张朴素的床，一个放得满满的书架，一个能在晚上眺望星空的窗户，一张书桌。 比如曾经看过一本漫画书（忘记叫什么名字了），讲一个老爷爷和他的小精灵生活在一个农场里。那个小精灵的房子在田地的中央，每天晚上他就爬上去，打开昏黄的台灯，就着星光读书。

又比如里约大冒险里那个小男孩，住在深巷中的一个房子的天台上，甚至不能叫屋子，只是雨棚下的简单铺成的床。每天晚上，他听着巷子里的人声喧闹，爬着水管回到家中，从破烂的箱子里拿出一杯饮料，坐下来看星星看月亮。

而大学一学期的时光里，我最享受的时刻就是上完了课去完了cca回到宿舍里，洗完澡之后坐下来，煮一碗泡面，看着窗外的星光，伏案学习。这样的时光里，我感受不到时间的流逝。人生的享受不过如此。

在上大学之前我从来没住过宿，也从来没有远离过家乡，有时候一个人呆着难免会觉得孤独。当我试图通过互联网转移注意力、试图在无意义的社交中浪费时间，孤独感并不会得到缓解。唯有在这种夜深人静的时刻，吹灭读书灯一身都是月的时候，我才能清清楚楚明明白白地感受到生命的存在，感受到意义，消解孤独。

突然又想到一句话，好像和以上没什么联系：人的一生，苦也罢，乐也罢，的也罢，失也罢，要紧的是心中要有一泓清泉不能没有月辉。

entry.title: Notes for SICPJS
entry.content:
Notes for SICP JS.

Introduction

SICPJS is a Java Script version(or Source Language) of the computer science textbook Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP) . The original textbook was written by Massachusetts Institute of Technology professors Harold Abelson and Gerald Jay Sussman with Julie Sussman, and is known as the "Wizard Book" in hacker culture. In NUS, year1 CS students who take CS1101S uses SICPJS as the reference book on their amazing study journey with the Source Academy.

During my study of CS in 2023/24 Sem 1 in CS1101, I find SICPJS a great book. It not only works as an introductory book to computer science, but also inspired me to think about the fundamental principles of CS. I wrote this note during my sem1 study in CS1101S and is still working on the book after that. Hopefully, this note would have been finished in 2023/24 Sem2.

This note involves ideas that I find inspiring in the book and questions that I came up with while reading. Notes with (U) are questions that are unsolved and it would be great if readers could comment below about your ideas on the notes.

I recommend anyone studying CS to read the book(either the original version or JS version), and hopefully this note would be of some use to the reader's understanding of the book.

Notes

Foreword

  1. (U)What is combinatory calculus & lambda calculus & predicate calculus?

    Lambda Calculus : (lambda x.lambda y. x+y)(5 6) = 11

    A lambda notation is equivalent to a Turing Machine, vice versa

  2. (U)How can you use a lambda notation to represent AND and OR?

    For reference, if we define True and False as:

     True: lambda x. lambda y. x

 False: lambda x. lambda y. y

    For any boolean value b, the logic NOT can be represented as

     NOT: lambda b. b False True

    Proof: case 1: b = True

             Not b = Not True = True Flase True = False

     case2: b = False

         Not b = Not False = False False True = True

  3. (U)What is object oriented approach?

  4. (U)Why does the author say that most programming languages “does not have a completely automatic storage management”?

  5. (U)Is it better to have 100 functions programmed on 1 data structure or 10 functions for each of 10 data structures?

  6. (U)What is Lisp?

Chapter 1 Building Abstractions with Functions

1.1

Inflix Notation

operand + operator + operand

JS programmers know the value of everything but the cost of nothing.(LOL)

The fact that every expression has a value and JS programmers are known for not caring about time/space efficiency.

(U)What is the difference between statements and expressions?

A?

Snake Case & Camel Case

Different ways to name variables.

Chapter 3: Modularity, Objects, and State

3.1

Why use the CSE machine, why is environment an important thing?(3.1.2)

Storing the temporary data that the function needs in the local environment of a function helps us to generalize functions(for example, in this way the memoization progress can be applied to different functions)

Encapsulation: We say a variable is encapsulated in the environment when its information is only accessed inside the environment. A general systemword designing principle called “the hiding principle“ is that a system gets more modular and robust by providing information access to only the parts of the programme that are “need to know”. 

Monte Carlo Method

The use of randomness. Say you want to calculate the area of a circle(x^2 +y^2 <= 1). Choose a rectangle(whose area you have already know), and try to make many following experiments: pick up one point and test whether the point is in the circle. If we try this many times, we get the possibility of a point in the rectangle is in the circle, thus p * area_of_rectangle = area_of_circle.

entry.title: 我的世界起床战争技巧总结
entry.content:
我的世界起床战争技巧总结

PVP

控距

将敌人保持在自己攻击范围的边上。为了保持这一点，手可以时不时按w而不是一直按着键盘

攻击

跳劈可以用，但是容易翻车。可以尝试在第一次攻击的时候跳劈，注意往旁边跳劈会比较好一点。

搭路

找到节奏感
